exercises.title: The three parts of the ReAct loop
exercises.prompt: Every 2026 agent is a variant of the ReAct loop. Name the three parts (Thought, Action, Observation) and explain why each is load-bearing — what breaks if you remove it.
exercises.hint: Thought = reasoning about what to do next; Action = a tool call; Observation = the tool's result fed back. Remove Thought → no planning; remove Action → can't affect the world; remove Observation → acts blind, can't adapt.
exercises.solution:
**The three parts:**
- **Thought:** the model **reasons about what to do next** given the goal and what it has observed so far — 'I need the user's city before I can get the weather'. It's the planning/decision step.
- **Action:** the model **emits a tool call** (Phase 13.1's decide step) — the only way it affects the world. 'call get_weather(city)'.
- **Observation:** the host **executes the action and feeds the result back** — 'get_weather returned 18°C'. The model reads it and loops.
These **interleave** — Thought → Action → Observation → Thought → ... — until a **stop condition** fires (task done, max steps, error).

**Why each is load-bearing:**
- **Remove Thought** → no reasoning between actions; the agent **can't plan, decompose, or decide which tool to use** — it degenerates into blind tool-firing. Thought is where the intelligence lives (and modern models do this as native reasoning, see below).
- **Remove Action** → the model **can't affect the world** — it's back to a text generator that can describe but not do (Phase 13.1's 'LLM produces tokens, program takes actions'). Action is the bridge to the real world.
- **Remove Observation** → the agent **acts blind** — it never sees the results of its actions, so it **can't adapt, recover from errors, or build on prior steps**. It would just guess the next action with no feedback. Observation closes the loop, turning open-loop guessing into closed-loop, fact-grounded action.
Together they form a **closed feedback loop**: reason → act → observe → react. This is the irreducible core of every agent, from the 2022 ReAct paper to Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, and Operator in 2026.

exercises.title: Implement a minimal agent loop
exercises.prompt: Implement a stdlib agent loop with a toy LLM, a tool registry, and a stop condition (under ~200 lines conceptually). Explain the stop condition and why a max-steps cap is mandatory.
exercises.hint: Loop: build prompt from history → LLM emits thought + action (or 'finish') → execute action via registry → append observation → repeat. Stop when the model emits a final answer OR max_steps hit. Max-steps prevents infinite loops.
exercises.solution:
```python
def agent_loop(llm, tools, goal, max_steps=10):
    history = [{"role": "user", "content": goal}]
    for step in range(max_steps):                 # MAX-STEPS cap (mandatory)
        response = llm(history)                     # THOUGHT + ACTION (or finish)
        if response.get("finish"):                  # STOP CONDITION: final answer
            return response["answer"]
        action = response["action"]                 # ACTION: a tool call
        tool = tools.get(action["name"])
        if tool is None:
            obs = {"error": f"unknown tool: {action['name']}"}
        else:
            try:
                obs = tool(**action["args"])        # EXECUTE
            except Exception as e:
                obs = {"error": str(e)}             # errors as observations
        history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response["raw"]})
        history.append({"role": "tool",                                  # OBSERVATION
                        "content": str(obs)})
    return "Stopped: max steps reached."            # safety backstop
```
The loop: **build the prompt from history → the LLM emits a thought + an action (or signals finish) → execute the action via the tool registry → append the observation → repeat**. Each iteration is one Thought-Action-Observation cycle.

**The stop condition:** the loop ends when **either** (a) the **model signals it's done** (emits a final answer — the natural completion) **or** (b) the **max-steps cap is hit** (a safety backstop). The model decides *when the task is complete*; the cap decides *when to give up*.

**Why a max-steps cap is mandatory:** without it, an agent can **loop forever** — it gets stuck retrying a failing tool, oscillates between actions, or never decides it's done (Phase 13.9's infinite-loop risk). Each loop iteration is an **expensive LLM call** (tokens, latency, cost — Phase 11.11), so an uncapped loop can **burn unbounded money and time** and hang the system. The max-steps cap is the **non-negotiable safety backstop** — it guarantees the loop terminates. Production agents also add per-loop **token/cost/time budgets** and **repeated-action detection** on top. The cap doesn't fix the underlying problem (a stuck agent), but it **bounds the damage** — which is why every harness has one. (Modern harnesses — Claude Agent SDK, OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph, AutoGen — all run this same loop with these same guards under the hood; learning it cold means you understand every framework's core.)

exercises.title: Prompt-based thought vs native reasoning, and why the loop persists
exercises.prompt: Explain the 2026 shift from prompt-based thought tokens to native model reasoning (Responses API, encrypted reasoning passthrough), what it changes about the loop, and why every modern harness still runs the ReAct loop underneath despite reasoning models. What are the loop's enduring limits?
exercises.hint: Old: 'Thought:' tokens prompted in the context (visible CoT). New: models reason natively (reasoning is internal/encrypted, passed between turns via the API). The loop (reason→act→observe) is unchanged; only WHERE thought happens moved. Limits: long-horizon, error recovery, context, cost.
exercises.solution:
**The 2026 shift (prompt-based thought → native reasoning):**
- **Old (prompt-based thought tokens):** you **prompted the model to emit 'Thought:' tokens** in the context before each action (the original ReAct prompting). The reasoning was **visible text in the prompt**, generated as part of the completion — you engineered the CoT explicitly (Phase 11.2).
- **New (native model reasoning):** **reasoning models** (o-series, Claude with extended thinking, Phase 9.12) **reason natively** — the model does its 'thinking' internally as part of inference, not as prompted text you wrote. The **Responses API** and **encrypted reasoning passthrough** let the model's reasoning be **carried between turns** (passed along, often encrypted, so the model retains its chain of thought across the loop) **without you managing it as visible prompt text**. The reasoning is **first-class in the model/API**, not bolted on via prompting.

**What it changes about the loop:** **only *where* the Thought happens moved** — from **prompted text you engineer** to **native model inference the API manages**. The loop structure is **unchanged**: it's still **reason (now native) → act (tool call) → observe (result) → repeat**. You no longer hand-write 'Thought:' prompts; the model reasons natively and you pass its reasoning state through the API. But the **three-part loop is identical** — native reasoning is a *better implementation of the Thought step*, not a replacement for the loop.

**Why every modern harness still runs the ReAct loop underneath:** the loop is the **fundamental structure of agency** — to act in the world, an agent **must** reason about what to do (Thought), do it (Action), and see what happened (Observation), iterating until done. **No amount of model improvement removes this** — even a perfect reasoning model still needs to **call tools and observe results** to affect the world (an LLM alone produces only tokens, Phase 13.1). So **Claude Agent SDK, OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph, AutoGen v0.4** — all of them — run **reason → act → observe → repeat** under the hood; reasoning models just make the 'reason' step better. The loop is the **load-bearing abstraction** that every framework, harness, and product (Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, Operator) is a variant of.

**The loop's enduring limits:** the basic loop works, but it **doesn't by itself solve**:
- **Long-horizon tasks:** over many steps, **errors compound** and the agent loses the thread (the long-horizon reliability problem, Phase 12.25's ~30% on hard benchmarks). The loop has no inherent planning or recovery.
- **Error recovery:** the basic loop reacts to observations but doesn't reliably **detect and recover from its own mistakes** — it can continue on a broken state.
- **Context management:** history grows unboundedly; the loop has no built-in **memory or context management** (needs MemGPT-style paging, Lesson 7).
- **Cost/latency:** every step is an LLM call — long loops are expensive and slow.
- **No verification:** the agent **marks its own work done** (the stop condition is the model's say-so) — unreliable without external verification (Lessons 38-39).
These limits are **why the rest of Phase 14 exists** — the loop is the foundation, but reliable agents need **planning (Lessons 2, 11), reflection (Lessons 3-5), memory (Lessons 7-9), the workbench (instructions, scope, verification, review — Lessons 31-42)** layered on top. Master the loop cold (it's universal), but know it's the *start*, not the whole story.
